posts.name: Ramon Cabrera
posts.cooked:
Bordeaux 2001s @ Marta (NYC)
February 15, 2022

Smaller attendees than our normal batch of regulars, 6 of us had dinner of all-shared, but with multiple orders of, house-cured pancetta, crocchette, 3 kinds of pizza, pork chop and Florentine steak. By the looks of the cleaned-up serving plates, I’d say all enjoyed the dishes. I also find that the smaller than normal group was a welcome respite as we all pretty much were able to converse at more than one point with one another.

Bordeaux 2001 was supposed to be a wine-theme dinner slated with this group last year. However, due to various limitations with the Covid-19 pandemic we postponed as we, instead, went ahead with the theme on its 21st vintage anniversary.

2001 Domaine de Chevalier Blanc, Pessac-Leognan
The usual and normal Bordeaux blanc wood component is present, but so are good levels of acidity and lemony components cored by nice plump fruit. I heard some say that this was a little advanced, but I’m not complaining as it did drink well for me. B+

2001 Ch. Pavie, Saint Emilion
I sensed that this was quite a highly anticipated wine by a few in the table. This certainly leaned on the modern end of my personal Bordeaxu-meter, but I found it to be relaxed and restrained in its structure and balance. It certainly was not over-the-top, a medium to full bodied effort, very much a yes on the ripe red fruit, some fresh ground pepper notes, and there’s sufficient Bordeaux qualities for me to say that I would not refuse it if a glass was poured for me. Quality is obviously up there. B/B+

2001 Ch. Trotanoy, Pomerol
This was very Pomerol and in line with traditional style with earth and wet leaves on the bouquet. Cool-ripened black fruit, leather, herbs and a lovely balance upon taking a few sips. My kind of Bordeaux. A-

2001 Ch. Pichon-Longueville Baron, Pauillac
There was tobacco, menthol on the bouquet. Some hard-edged tannin in a rich taste and texture with fruit that seemed a little too lean for my taste. Finish seemed clipped at this point. B/B-

2001 Ch. Pichon-Longueville Comtesse de Lalande, Pauillac
Leather, gravel and leather on the nose. Finesse texture with presence of cool-weather ripe red fruit, herbs and an overall inviting structure. Lengthy finish. At a nice drinking stage with a 1-hour double decant, but don’t be afraid to hold out for 10, 20 years more. A-

2001 Ch. Haut Brion, Pessac-Leognan
The structure presents itself very well with more cool-weather ripeness, elegant rather than brawny, but definitely young to my taste. Wonderfully resonated and enjoyably lingered after each sip. The future is very bright. B+/A-

2001 Ch. La Mission Haut Brion, Pessac-Leognan
I did not taste after most around had declared the bottle as corked. NR

2001 Ch. Smith Haut Lafitte, Pessac-Leognan
A subbed bottle after the LMHB was declared unfit. The most modern in the nose and taste categories with all the bottles that were drank tonight. I don’t think it pretends to be not modern as it showcased sweetness and ripeness at a very high level and the mocha notes just seem to persist. A treat for those who are lovers of this style. C+/B-

posts.name: Dale Williams
posts.cooked:
Thanks Ramon. I liked the SHL more than you, generous of you to open another bottle. And liked Baron more. My take

Tuesday I tool the train in and met a nice crew at Marta. . We shared pancetta (with sauteed radicchio and chicory, sunchokes), crocchette with olive aioli, 3 pizzas (stracciatella, “butcher”, and Brussels sprouts/ham)\ then split a pork chop and a steak Florentine.

2001 Domaine de Chevalier Blanc
I always struggle with aeration on these wines (and know white Bdx premoxes at similar rates to Burgundy). I chose to open this at table. Fresh citrus and tropical nose, though on the palate there’s a woody note with some oxidative flavors. Revisited later, it seemed fresher, honied but bright, wax, pear, and melon. B+ at end. Should have decanted.

2001 Ch Pavie
Ripe, really ripe, but only moderately woody, reminds me more of the ‘98 than the ‘00 or 03. Black plum cocoa, mocha. Some others liked more. B-/B

2001 Ch. Trotanoy
This was more subdued than a bottle in the fall, some tannic structure, red plum fruit with a touch of mocha, moderate acids. Seemed a bit in a shell, but stylistically more my style than the Pavie. Give it time, better at end. B+/A-

2001 Ch. Pichon Baron
Leather and cedar. Powerful and brawny B+/A-
2001 Ch. Pichon Lalande
Young, elegant, and long. Cassis accented with green herbs and anise, mellow tannins, lively For drinking now (though with long future) my WOTN. A-

2001 La Mission Haut Brion
Corked. The pain, the pain.

2001 Ch. Haut Brion
This was a little angry and sullen at being awakened. More tannic than I expected, but beautiful bones underneath. Eventually it begins to show more- black currant and black cherry, campfire, earth. B+/B this night, A potential.

2001 Ch. Smith Haut-Lafitte
Quite ripe, quite modern, but still Bordeaux. Cassis, chocolate, but with a Graves-y rock undertone. B

Really fun night, all wines really seemed on young side, such a shame about the La Mish (which was my WOTN last time we did 2001s)

Grade disclaimer: I’m a very easy grader, basically A is an excellent wine, B a good wine, C drinkable. Anything below C means I wouldn’t drink at a party where it was only choice. Furthermore, I offer no promises of objectivity, accuracy, and certainly not of consistency.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked: Thanks for the notes. It was always in the shadow of the 2000, and there has been some reassessment particularly for Saint Emilion. I have had Trotanoy twice in the last couple of years, and really liked, but both times it was in the same flight as the 1998, which is a truly extraordinary wine and will dwarf everything around it.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked: Rock Star Ramon! Great notes. I love this vintage. Had that 2001 Trotanoy a few weeks ago next to an 85. Love this Chateau! Wish I had more of that 2001, it’s wonderful.

posts.name: Jon Lawrence
posts.cooked: After reading the notes on the '01 Baron, I would like to try that next to the '00 and then maybe a glass of them blended together to see if 1+1=3.

posts.name: Steve Costigan
posts.cooked:
Great notes from a vintage that has climbed in my estimation each time I’ve opened a bottle as the years go on. Gracias.

There’s been talk on WB about how glacially slow Dom de Chevalier blanc is to mature and the 2001 is especially slow. I had the 2001 in 2017 and it drank like it was bottled six months prior. It could be a 30-40 year wine??

I’ve still got a few bottles of 2001 Pichon Lalande, Montrose, Cos and Pontet Canet and am greatly looking forward to resampling but not any time soon.

posts.name: Paul Jaouen
posts.cooked:
After reading the notes on the '01 Baron, I would like to try that next to the '00 and then maybe a glass of them blended together to see if 1+1=3.

Good idea! I was more in Dale’s camp. I liked the Baron quite a bit.

posts.name: Pat_Martin
posts.cooked:
Thanks for the check up. I only have the 01 Pichon Lalande cellared from your line up, so I’m certainly pleased by its showing.

I tried a couple of 01 Pomerols last October (VCC, Certan de May) that were tasty but still seemed young.

posts.name: J D o v e
posts.cooked: 2001 is a birth year for my eldest daughter. I love 2001 PLL — Trotenoy too….

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked:
That corked bottle hurts

Normally I don’t like blanc Graves…but the 2001’s were better than other years I thought. Sounds like the DdC was quite solid.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: Still have close to 50 bottles of various 2001s. Have a couple ready to go soon.

posts.name: Ramon Cabrera
posts.cooked:
That corked bottle hurts

Normally I don’t like blanc Graves…but the 2001’s were better than other years I thought. Sounds like the DdC was quite solid.

Yes, the DdC blanc did not disappoint, at least not for me.

Btw, we had learned to live with off-bottles given the frequent aged themes that this group indulges on. Even as far back to the days when you and MattR were getting these dinners off the ground, more than a decade ago .

I happen to have a themed bottle, SHL, in my very limited-volume home wine-fridge that I brought along with the PLL just in case things turn out for the worse with one or more of our bottles. Will not have made up for the what-ifs with LMHB, but kept wine flow continuity

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Thanks for the notes. It was always in the shadow of the 2000, and there has been some reassessment particularly for Saint Emilion. I have had Trotanoy twice in the last couple of years, and really liked, but both times it was in the same flight as the 1998, which is a truly extraordinary wine and will dwarf everything around it.

Well, I think it’s clear that 2001 is better than 2000 in plenty of cases (beyond the case of St Emilion); but initial vintage impressions are so hard to shake in Bordeaux that the market hasn’t really caught up. 2001 Léoville-Poyferré is one example that is drinking incredibly well from my cellar right now.

posts.name: David_Bloch
posts.cooked: 2001 VCC. Just terrific at the moment. Certainly could do with some more time, but lovely. A touch sweet on the nose - quite floral and almost hoisin. This a big and deep 2001. Maybe some tobacco and wet earth. Definitely leans to the red side on the palate. The wine still needs more time to reach the heights that I think it will reach, but if you’ve got a number of bottles this is a real treat today. Seriously impressive.

posts.name: J0shu@ Durh@m
posts.cooked:
Had the '01 PLL night. Very enjoyable but if you don’t like herbaceous bdx you won’t care for this. But it’s no refined and elegant. Why I love PLL!

  • 2001 Château Pichon Longueville Comtesse de Lalande - France, Bordeaux, Médoc, Pauillac (12/13/2025)
    7yrs from my last bottle. SUPER green upon opening. Crazy eucalyptus that did dissipate and integrate witha few hours air. There’s moderate bricking on the edges. Very expressive nose of mint/eucalyptus, tobacco leaf and cigar box notes. Palate is a little lean, but fine mature fruit elements. Quite poised and focused. Lengthy finish. Classic and delicious.
    Given the rather lean narrow palate and mature fruit elements I have a hard time seeing this improve further—but certainly in no danger of fading anytime soon. (94 points)

Posted from CellarTracker

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

Well, I think it’s clear that 2001 is better than 2000 in plenty of cases (beyond the case of St Emilion); but initial vintage impressions are so hard to shake in Bordeaux that the market hasn’t really caught up.

Agree 100%.

Love some of those wines popped above. Trotanoy and VCC are excellent. Heck, the lowly Sociando 2001 way over-performed its 2000 counterpart.

posts.name: Jack Bierly
posts.cooked: I always have thought of PLL having that green streak - stylistically I wonder if they have changed. I did drink a 2020 a year back and didn’t pick up on it.

posts.name: Ramon Cabrera
posts.cooked: I’m nowhere near the Bordeaux pro-level that William and Robert are, but my less than qualified recent experiences is starting to cement my earlier views that Bordeaux vintage 2000 is mostly and generally lording it over their 2001 counterparts.

posts.name: Yule Kim
posts.cooked:
JBierly:

I always have thought of PLL having that green streak - stylistically I wonder if they have changed. I did drink a 2020 a year back and didn’t pick up on it.

I always found the PLL green streak to be a feature, not a bug.

I also agree that its less prevalent in more recent vintages of PLL. Sic transit gloria mundi.

posts.name: Jack Bierly
posts.cooked: Well as I have learned, it is the methoxypyrazines that lead to it - considered an attribute for some palates and a flaw for others - but the main thing is canopy management can alter it. So just curious if PLL has changed their viticulture? Whatever, I tend to not like it so traditionally have preferred PB over PLL but like them both!
